did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper developed an Intelligent Multilevel Thresholding with Deep Learning (IMLT-DL) model for skin lesion segmentation and classification using dermoscopic images. The method employed Inception v3 for feature extraction and a Gradient Boosting Tree (GBT) model for classification, achieving an accuracy of 0.992 on the ISIC dataset.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work developing a new 'Intelligent Multilevel Thresholding with Deep Learning (IMLT-DL)' model for skin lesion segmentation and classification. It details specific methods (Top hat filtering, Mayfly Optimization, Inception v3, GBT), describes the implementation on the ISIC dataset, and reports original experimental results including accuracy (0.992), sensitivity, and specificity metrics, comparing them against existing methods.

title: Deep Learning-Based Skin Lesion Diagnosis Model Using Dermoscopic Images
brief_summary: This study developed an Intelligent Multilevel Thresholding with Deep Learning (IMLT-DL) model for skin lesion segmentation and classification using dermoscopic images to address challenges like artifacts and variable lesion shapes. The methodology employed Top hat filtering and inpainting for pre-processing, Mayfly Optimization with multilevel Kapur’s thresholding for segmentation, Inception v3 for feature extraction, and a Gradient Boosting Tree (GBT) for classification. The model was evaluated on the International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC) dataset against various existing methods. Experimental results demonstrated that the proposed IMLT-DL model achieved a maximum accuracy of 0.992, with a sensitivity of 0.970 and specificity of 0.995. These outcomes indicate that the IMLT-DL model outperforms existing techniques in effectively classifying different skin lesion types.

all_task_types.target: Infected skin lesion regions in dermoscopic images
all_task_types.details: The paper employs Mayfly Optimization (MFO) with multilevel Kapur’s thresholding to segment skin lesions from dermoscopic images, determining the infected regions.

all_task_types.target: Discrete diagnostic categories of skin lesions (Angioma, Nevus, Lentigo NOS, Solar Lentigo, Melanoma, Seborrheic Keratosis, Basal Cell Carcinoma)
all_task_types.details: The primary task is multiclass classification of skin lesions using a Gradient Boosting Tree (GBT) model fed with features extracted by Inception v3. The model predicts discrete class labels for 7 specific lesion types on the ISIC dataset, achieving an accuracy of 0.992.

datasets_used.name: International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC) dataset
datasets_used.description: A public dataset comprising dermoscopic images used for skin lesion segmentation and classification. The images in the dataset are in the size of 640 * 480 pixels.

use.training: Used for training the Gradient Boosting Tree (GBT) model via XGBoost with features extracted by Inception v3.
use.testing: Used for testing the proposed IMLT-DL model to evaluate performance metrics such as accuracy, sensitivity, and specificity.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To evaluate classification performance metrics (sensitivity, specificity, accuracy, precision, G-measure) for each skin lesion class and compare the proposed IMLT-DL model against existing methods.
To assess the diagnostic ability of the IMLT-DL model in distinguishing between classes, reporting a maximum ROC value of 98.765.
To compare the accuracy, sensitivity, and specificity of the proposed IMLT-DL model against existing methods (SVM, CNN, DL-ANFC, etc.) using reported experimental values.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, precision, G-measure, and ROC analysis, with comparisons made based on these metric values against existing methods.

ISIC dataset.notes: The IMLT-DL model was evaluated on 310 images across 7 classes: Angioma (20), Nevus (44), Lentigo NOS (39), Solar Lentigo (67), Melanoma (50), Seborrheic Keratosis (52), and Basal Cell Carcinoma (37). The model outperformed existing methods in accuracy, sensitivity, and specificity.

architecture_used.main_model: Intelligent Multilevel Thresholding with Deep Learning (IMLT-DL)

pipeline_stages: Pre-processing: Top hat filtering and inpainting
Segmentation: Mayfly Optimization (MFO) with multilevel Kapur’s thresholding

feature_extractor.details: Used to derive feature vectors from segmented images. Utilizes fundamental convolution block, enhanced Inception block, and classification block. Employs 1x1 convolution kernels to reduce feature channel count and computational complexity.

classifier.details: Trained in an additive way; grows another tree at every time step to minimize residuals. Non-variant to input scaling. Objective function includes loss function and regularization norm.

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: Intelligent Multilevel Thresholding with Deep Learning (IMLT-DL)
preprocessing_techniques: Hair removal using Top hat filtering to identify thick and dark hair
Inpainting (painting process) to replace hairline pixels with nearby pixel values
conclusions: The proposed Intelligent Multilevel Thresholding with Deep Learning (IMLT-DL) model effectively addresses challenges in skin lesion segmentation and classification, such as artifacts (hair, gel bubbles), blurry boundaries, and variable lesion shapes.
The IMLT-DL model achieved a maximum accuracy of 0.992, sensitivity of 0.970, and specificity of 0.995 on the ISIC dataset, outperforming existing methods including SVM, CNN, DL-ANFC, and ResNets.
The model demonstrated high performance across seven specific skin lesion classes: Angioma, Nevus, Lentigo NOS, Solar Lentigo, Melanoma, Seborrheic Keratosis, and Basal Cell Carcinoma.
The IMLT-DL model achieved a maximum ROC value of 98.765, indicating strong diagnostic ability in distinguishing between different skin lesion types.
The study concludes that the IMLT-DL model is an appropriate tool for segmenting and classifying skin lesions using dermoscopic images in real-time environments.
important_remarks: The study uses a very small dataset of only 310 dermoscopic images from the ISIC dataset, with highly imbalanced class sizes (e.g., Angioma n=20 vs. Solar Lentigo n=67), which limits the generalizability and statistical robustness of the results.
The paper does not report formal statistical hypothesis testing, p-values, or confidence intervals to support the claim that the proposed IMLT-DL model significantly outperforms existing methods.
There is no mention of external validation; the model was evaluated solely on the ISIC dataset, raising concerns about potential dataset bias and overfitting to this specific collection.

The paper does not specify the train/validation/test split ratios or the cross-validation strategy used, making it difficult to assess data leakage risks or the reliability of the reported accuracy (0.992).
Data augmentation techniques were not employed, which may contribute to the risk of overfitting given the small sample size.
The authors declared no specific funding and no conflicts of interest, but the lack of open-source code or detailed hyperparameter settings limits reproducibility.
The claim that the model is suitable for 'real-time environments' is not supported by computational complexity metrics (e.g., inference time, FLOPs) in the provided text.
